Maybe You Should Know a Bit About CI/CD: Deploying to Render.com with CD
Knowing a bit about CI/CD is genuinely useful as a developer. Even on a small side project, CI/CD pays for itself — and even if you're "just a frontend dev", there's plenty you can do with it.
First — what are CI and CD?
OK, you might already be thinking:
"What exactly are we 'continuously' integrating and deploying?"
So let me paint a picture.
When you deploy a project to production, you usually run a few commands —
to bundle, then ship the build artifacts. That's a normal dev workflow.

Looks fine on paper. But humans are forgetful — skip the tests one time, forget to rebuild another time. Skipping tests is especially bad: best case, you patch in prod immediately. Worst case, your phone rings.
That's what CI fixes — it automates the stuff you should be doing anyway. Tests, builds, etc.
CD? That's about deployment. CD auto-deploys for you, which saves time and removes one more thing you can mess up.
With the concepts out of the way — what CI/CD tool to use? A few options:
This post uses GitHub Actions, since most of us are already on GitHub and GitHub Actions is first-party.
Before touching GitHub Actions, you need one thing:
files. That's the format GitHub Actions configs use — what jobs to run, what to test, build, deploy. (
are the same format, different extension.)
for multiple values (like an array), or
is just more compact. For objects, use
preserves line breaks across multiple lines,
joins them on a single line.
Under the hood YAML is essentially a flavor of JSON. If you know JSON, YAML should click pretty fast. Side-by-side examples below.
language-yml shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast
language-json shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast
name: {{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }} # automatically pulls the GitHub token

name:
  firstName: Ray
  lastName: IsRayNotArray
  age: 18

name:
  - firstName: Ray
    lastName: IsRayNotArray
    age: 18
  - firstName: Ray
    lastName: IsRayNotArray
    age: 18
Now that YAML's behind us, time for GitHub Actions itself.
Heads up — there's no UI editor. You define everything in

name: learn-github-actions
run-name: ${{ github.actor }} is learning GitHub Actions
on: [push]
jobs:
  check-bats-version:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v3
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v3
        with:
          node-version: '14'
      - run: npm install -g bats
      - run: bats -v
Don't panic — going line by line.

name: learn-github-actions
run-name: ${{ github.actor }} is learning GitHub Actions
is the workflow name — what shows up in the GitHub Actions tab.
is the description for each run, and
is a variable for the GitHub username — so it reads "Ray is learning GitHub Actions" or whatever.
trigger — this workflow fires when you push.

jobs:
  check-bats-version:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v3
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v3
        with:
          node-version: '14'
      - run: npm install -g bats
      - run: bats -v
is what runs when the event fires.
lines are the bit people stumble on. What's
lets your workflow fetch your code.
lets you pick a Node.js version.
syntax should make a bit more sense now.
block — that's where you pass arguments.
sets Node.js to v14. There are more options for
, but most of the time the version is all you need. See the
— those just run shell commands, the same way you would locally.

With the fundamentals down, time to write some real workflows.
I'll use an Express skeleton I've prepared:
. GitHub Actions configs have to live there. Inside, create
. Your structure should look like this:
filename doesn't matter — it's just for your own organization. I'll use
, so you can use that too).
The first three run on pull requests. Deploy runs on merges to the main branch. So two workflow files:
. We want the workflow to fire on

name: Main Pull Requests Check
on:
  pull_request:
is written as an object because it accepts a lot of sub-properties. See the
for the full list. The most common one is
— which target branch the PR is going into:

name: Main Pull Requests Check
on:
  pull_request:
    branches:
      - main
Means: "fire this workflow when someone opens a PR against
Inline array form works the same:

name: Main Pull Requests Check
on:
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]
Wildcards work for Git Flow-style branch names like

name: Main Pull Requests Check
on:
  pull_request:
    branches: [feature/*]
Event done — onto the Job. We want three steps inside one job: ESLint, tests, build. For ESLint + tests we need both packages:
language-bash shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast
Initialize ESLint for the project and write a basic test — I'll skip the setup details. To let GitHub Actions invoke them, add two scripts in
runs Jest. With those in place, back to the workflow.

name: Main Pull Requests Check
on:
  pull_request:
    branches:
      - main
jobs:
  lint:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v3
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v3
        with:
          node-version: '16'
      - run: npm install
      - run: npm run lint
If ESLint finds any error, the job fails and you'll see it on the Actions tab:
Once you fix it, the job passes:
Tests and build are basically the same pattern. Since this is an Express project there's no build step, so I'll skip it below:

name: Main Pull Requests Check
on:
  pull_request:
    branches:
      - main
jobs:
  lint:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v3
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v3
        with:
          node-version: '16'
      - run: npm install
      - run: npm run lint
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v3
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v3
        with:
          node-version: '16'
      - run: npm install
      - run: npm run test
repeat across jobs. Why? Because each job runs in its own container — independent environment, fresh state. So each needs its own setup steps.
Also worth knowing — GitHub Actions doesn't guarantee job order by default. ESLint might run before tests, or after. If you want a specific order, use

name: Main Pull Requests Check
on:
  pull_request:
    branches:
      - main
jobs:
  lint:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v3
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v3
        with:
          node-version: '16'
      - run: npm install
      - run: npm run lint
  test:
    needs:
      - lint
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v3
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v3
        with:
          node-version: '16'
      - run: npm install
      - run: npm run test
, the Actions visualization looks like this:
The visualization lives under the project's Actions tab.

name: Main Deploy
on:
  push:
    branches:
      - main
That's it for the event side. Easy.
Where to deploy? I'll demo Render.com, since Render exposes a

You might ask — why bother with CD when Render can auto-deploy on branch push directly? In some setups you specifically need your CI/CD pipeline to finish before deploy can happen (run all the tests first, etc.), so doing it explicitly with CI/CD gives you that control. That's why we're going this route instead of letting Render handle the branch watching.
Some setup first. When you create a Render project,
— otherwise Render will deploy before your CI even finishes:
For an existing project, do this in the project's Settings:
Right next to that is the
URL — copy it. Anyone with this URL can trigger a deploy of your project, so keep it secret.
Now grab an API key. Click your avatar → "Account Setting":
Find "API Keys" and click "Create API Key":
Name it whatever — I used "Example". You'll get a key — save it, you'll need it.
Back to the workflow. Even though the PR already passed lint and tests, you might want to re-run them on main before deploying — extra paranoia:

name: Main Deploy
on:
  push:
    branches:
      - main
jobs:
  lint:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v3
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v3
        with:
          node-version: '16'
      - run: npm install
      - run: npm run lint
  test:
    needs:
      - lint
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v3
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v3
        with:
          node-version: '16'
      - run: npm install
      - run: npm run test
Time to add the deploy step. First, paste your Deploy Hook URL and API key into the repo's Secrets — Settings tab on GitHub:
so deploy only runs after lint and test pass:

name: Main Deploy
on:
  push:
    branches:
      - main
jobs:
  lint:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v3
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v3
        with:
          node-version: '16'
      - run: npm install
      - run: npm run lint
  test:
    needs:
      - lint
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v3
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v3
        with:
          node-version: '16'
      - run: npm install
      - run: npm run test
  deploy:
    needs:
      - lint
      - test
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
to hit the Render Deploy Hook:

name: Main Deploy
on:
  push:
    branches:
      - main
jobs:
  lint:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v3
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v3
        with:
          node-version: '16'
      - run: npm install
      - run: npm run lint
  test:
    needs:
      - lint
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v3
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v3
        with:
          node-version: '16'
      - run: npm install
      - run: npm run test
  deploy:
    needs:
      - lint
      - test
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Deploy
        run: |
          curl -X POST -H "Authorization: Bearer ${{ secrets.RENDER_TOKEN }}" \
          -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
          -d '{}' \
          ${{ secrets.RENDER_DEPLOY_HOOK }}
Open a PR, merge it to
, and GitHub Actions runs the chain — lint, test, deploy. Render takes the deploy hook ping and ships:

Knowing a bit about CI/CD is genuinely useful as a developer — even on a side project, you can put it to work. This post covers what CI/CD actually is, the YAML basics behind GitHub Actions, how workflows are structured (Event → Runner → Job → Step), and a full walkthrough of building a PR-check workflow (ESLint + Jest) and a deploy workflow that ships to Render.com via Deploy Hooks.
